title: The Anthropic Library
subtitle: 17 official skills: documents, design, development, and a meta-skill

theory.md:
Before writing your own skill, it helps to see how Anthropic itself makes them. The company has published an official library of skills in an open repository — **github.com/anthropics/skills** — with 17 skills, and you can open and read each one: they're ordinary folders with SKILL.md files.

For you, this library is three things at once. First, **ready-made tools**: many of these skills already work in Claude "out of the box" — for example, creating Word documents or Excel spreadsheets. Second, a **textbook**: the best examples of how to phrase instructions and descriptions. Third, a **source of starters**: the repository contains a `template/` folder — a template that's convenient to start your own skill from.

theory.text: Category 1: documents — docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx

theory.md:
The library's four "workhorses" are the skills for office files. They're exactly what fires when you ask Claude to produce not chat text, but a real file.

- **docx** — Word documents: reports, contracts, letters with headings, a table of contents, tables. It can both read existing .docx files and create new ones.
- **pdf** — working with PDFs: creating, extracting text and tables, merging and splitting files, filling out forms.
- **pptx** — PowerPoint presentations: slides with structure, layouts, speaker notes.
- **xlsx** — Excel spreadsheets: data, formulas, formatting, charts, cleaning up "messy" spreadsheets.

A real-life example: you say "put together a financial plan in Excel with formulas" — and Claude applies the xlsx skill, which describes inside how to build a workbook correctly: sheets, formulas instead of hardcoded numbers, formatting.

theory.text: Category 2: design — canvas-design, frontend-design, brand-guidelines, theme-factory

theory.md:
The second group is responsible for visual results.

- **canvas-design** — creating posters, covers, and static graphics (PNG/PDF) by design principles: composition, typography, color.
- **frontend-design** — quality web interfaces: it helps make sites and apps that look professional rather than "like a student project."
- **brand-guidelines** — Anthropic's brand style: the brand's colors and typography. Useful as a model: look at it to understand how to build a skill with **your** company's style.
- **theme-factory** — a set of ready-made design themes (colors + fonts) that can be applied to slides, documents, and pages, or generate a theme on the fly.

Alongside them in the library live **algorithmic-art** (generative art with p5.js code) and **slack-gif-creator** (animated GIFs for Slack) — more niche, but telling: even the "skill" of making GIFs can be a skill.

The third group is for those building software with Claude.

- **mcp-builder** — a guide to building MCP servers (integrations between Claude and external services) in Python or TypeScript. You'll come back to it in the world about MCP.
- **webapp-testing** — testing web applications: how to check that the app you built actually works.

Three more skills from the library sit next to development: **web-artifacts-builder** (complex interactive artifacts in React), **claude-api** (a reference for the Claude API: models, pricing, parameters), and a couple of "text" ones — **internal-comms** (internal communications: status updates, reports, announcements) and **doc-coauthoring** (co-authoring documentation and specs).

The library's most unusual skill is **skill-creator**: a skill that creates other skills. It knows the specification, asks the right questions about your task, and assembles a finished folder with a SKILL.md. It's your main helper in this world — lesson six is devoted to it.

What matters is that skill-creator is built the same way as the other sixteen: an ordinary folder with a SKILL.md. Open it in the repository — and you'll see that "creating skills" is described with the same means as "creating presentations": Markdown instructions. No magic, just well-packaged knowledge.

By category, then: 4 document + 6 design/media + 4 for development + 2 text + 1 meta = **17 skills**.

rows: "Make a report in Word," "put together a presentation"
canvas-design, frontend-design, brand-guidelines, theme-factory, algorithmic-art, slack-gif-creator
"Draw a poster," "format it in the brand colors"
"Write a status report," "help with the spec"

theory.bad: I need to make a presentation. First I'll explain to Claude what a .pptx is, what kinds of slide layouts there are, how to format headings… (half a page of instructions about things Claude already knows how to do)
theory.good: Put together an 8-slide presentation about the launch of our delivery service: for investors, business tone, a slide with numbers at the end. (The pptx skill loads on its own and takes on all the "how to make the file" work.)
theory.explanation: The official skills already contain expertise on formats — there's no need to explain to Claude how PowerPoint works. Your job is to give context and content requirements; the skill's job is the technical execution. A good request describes "what and for whom," not "how the file is built."

theory.md: In the README of the anthropics/skills repository, Anthropic describes it as an open collection of Agent Skills — examples of how to package expertise into a reusable form. Besides the skills themselves, it contains `spec/` (the format specification) and `template/` (a template for a new skill). The repository is open: skills can be read, forked, and adapted to your needs.

Don't memorize all 17 by heart — memorize the categories: documents, design, development, text, meta. And when you're writing your own skill, open a similar official one in the repository and peek at how its description and instructions are built. Learning from reference examples is legitimate and correct.

tasks.question: You ask Claude: "Turn this data into an Excel spreadsheet with formulas and a chart." Which official skill takes on the work?
tasks.explanation: Excel files are the xlsx skill's area of responsibility: data, formulas, formatting, charts. docx is for Word, pptx is for presentations, canvas-design is for posters and graphics.
tasks.hint: The extension of Excel files is .xlsx.

tasks.question: Which skills from the library belong to the "design" category? (select all that apply)
tasks.explanation: The design group: canvas-design (posters and graphics), theme-factory (design themes), brand-guidelines (brand style), plus frontend-design. mcp-builder is development (MCP servers), and xlsx is documents (Excel).
tasks.hint: The names of the design skills contain the words design, theme, brand.

tasks.question: Match the skill to the task: "I want Claude to help me write a skill for formatting our sales proposals."
options: skill-creator — the meta-skill that creates other skills
pdf — sales proposals are usually in PDF
tasks.explanation: The task is "create a skill," and the skill for creating skills is skill-creator. It knows the specification, will ask questions, and assemble a folder with a SKILL.md. The pdf skill would come in handy later — when the finished skill produces the proposals themselves.
tasks.hint: Look for "meta": the skill whose subject is skills themselves.

tasks.instruction: Assemble a request to Claude so that the official pptx skill works at its best: first context, then the task, then the content requirements. Technical "how to make the file" instructions are not your concern.
blocks: Context: I run a small coffee shop and I'm preparing a pitch to a potential partner.
Task: put together a presentation about our coffee shop and our plans to open a second location.
Requirements: 6–8 slides, business tone, and a table with the key numbers on the last slide.
distractors: Instruction: a .pptx file is a ZIP archive with XML markup, create slides via <p:sld> tags.
First explain to me in your own words what PowerPoint is.
tasks.explanation: A correct request is context + task + content requirements: everything the skill can't know about you. The trap blocks intrude into the skill's area of responsibility: the skill knows how the .pptx format is built better than you, and asking it to "explain what PowerPoint is" doesn't get you any closer to the result at all.

tasks.instruction: Practice choosing the tool. Describe to Claude a task that a document skill fits: ask for a finished Word file with a report (mention the format or the word "document/file" and what report it is).

steps.claudeReply:
Connecting the **docx** skill — it's responsible for Word files.

Document plan:
1. A title page with the report's name
2. A brief summary (half a page)
3. Main sections with headings and tables
4. Conclusions

Creating the file… Done: **report.docx**. Inside are heading styles, an auto-generated table of contents, and tables, not just text. The docx skill does all this by default — you didn't have to explain how Word is built.
steps.failHint: For example: "Prepare a quarterly sales report as a Word file (docx)" — the important thing is that it's clear: you need an actual document file.

tasks.successMessage: That's exactly how it works in real Claude: you name the result ("a Word file with a report"), and the right skill from the library loads on its own. Memorize the categories — and you'll always understand what's happening under the hood.
